summary: The author entered the competition late and adapted a spatio-temporal transformer architecture originally designed for 3D human motion prediction to predict player trajectories. The core innovation was a virtual pre/post pass-cut data augmentation technique that generated approximately 200k additional training samples, significantly improving model robustness. Training was stabilized using a modified TemporalHuber loss with time-decay weighting, and the final submission combined 48 models across different output horizons via a weighted mean. The author also noted that feature count yields diminishing returns and that validation metrics often misalign with the leaderboard, requiring careful trust calibration.
extensive_summary:
## Model Architecture
The solution centers on an **ST-transformer play-level model** adapted from a paper on 3D human motion prediction. The model takes input tensors of shape `(WINDOW, NUM_PLAYERS, FEATURES)` and predicts output shapes of `(HORIZON, NUM_PLAYERS, 2)`. Key hyperparameters include a hidden dimension of 64, 8 temporal and spatial attention heads, 4 layers, 0.05 dropout, and a feedforward dimension of 256. The author also injected edge biases (sameteam_ij, distance_ij, closing_speed_ij) into the spatial attention output, though noted the gain was marginal.

## Feature Engineering & Processing
Raw play-by-play data is transformed into a curated set of kinematic, spatial, and temporal features. The author observed diminishing returns from feature complexity and pruned the set down to basic relative distances, velocities, and frame-count context markers. Player role and position are embedded as 8-dimensional vectors. The most critical processing step is **virtual pre&post pass cut augmentation**, which shifts the pass event window forward or backward in time. This generates ~200k extra samples, with the pre-pass variant being crucial for exposing all player roles to the output targets. Augmented data is merged with original data and trained with a 0.5 loss weight.

## Training & Validation
Training utilizes a modified **TemporalHuber Loss** that applies a time-decay exponential weight (`exp(-time_decay * t)`) and masks invalid frames, which the author found highly stabilizing. Data is split by week across 18 weeks into 6 specific folds, with two random seeds per fold yielding 12 models per run. The author observed frequent misalignment between CV and LB scores, noting that late-stage CV improvements often indicate overfitting rather than genuine gains, and thus prioritized LB performance.

## Submission
The final submission aggregates **48 models** across 4 distinct training trials (each targeting different output horizons). Models are combined using a simple weighted mean to produce the final trajectory predictions.
competition_flow: Raw play-by-play data is processed into kinematic and spatial features, augmented via virtual pass-cut shifting to generate ~200k extra samples, fed into an ST-transformer trained with a time-decayed TemporalHuber loss, validated via a week-based 6-fold split, and submitted as a weighted ensemble of 48 models across different output horizons.
data_processing: Virtual pre/post pass cut augmentation generates ~200k additional samples
Augmented data merged with original data at a 0.5 loss weight
Feature set pruned to basic relative distances/velocities and frame counts
Player role and position embedded as 8-dim vectors
features_engineering: Ball metrics (ball_land_x/y, ball_dx/dy, distance_to_ball, ball_dir_x/y, ball_closing_speed)
cv_strategy: Week-based split across 18 weeks into 6 specific folds; two random seeds per fold; 12 models per training run.
ensembling: Final submission combined 48 models across 4 training trials (each with different output horizons) using a simple weighted mean.
insights: Adding features beyond a certain point yields diminishing returns, making basic relative distances and frame context sufficient.
Time-decay weighting in the TemporalHuber loss significantly stabilizes training dynamics.
Late-stage CV improvements often signal overfitting rather than genuine generalization, making LB trust more reliable.
Virtual pre-pass cut augmentation is critical because it forces the model to predict for all player roles, drastically improving robustness.
critical_findings: Virtual pre-pass cut augmentation is more impactful than post-pass cut because it forces the model to predict outputs for all player roles, not just the primary ones.
CV and LB scores frequently misalign (CV drops while LB rises), indicating that validation metrics can be misleading for this specific data distribution and time-split structure.

papers.title: A Spatio-temporal Transformer for 3D Human Motion Prediction
